steps.title: Getting Around Korea
steps.desc: Korea has world-class public transit. Subway, bus, KTX bullet train — all connected, cheap, on time. Every transport word is a decodable compound. Let's decode them.

steps.note:
COMPOUND: 지하 (underground) + 철 (iron/rail).
Literally: 'underground iron.'

지하: underground. 지 (earth) + 하 (below).
철: iron. Also in 철교 (iron bridge).

Seoul subway: 23 lines. Clean, frequent, cheap.
Wi-Fi throughout. Announcements in Korean + English.

Konglish: from English 'bus.'

버스 정류장: bus stop.
정류 (stop/stay) + 장 (place) = stop-place.

타다 = to ride / take (transport verb).

버스를 타다
→ to take the bus

택시를 타다
→ to take a taxi

지하철을 타다
→ to take the subway

기차: train.
SINO-KOREAN: 기 (machine) + 차 (vehicle).
기 reappears in: 비행기, 세탁기, 청소기.

KTX: Korea Train eXpress.
Seoul → Busan in 2.5 hours.
Seoul → Incheon airport 45 min.

steps.exampleEn: I go to Japan by airplane.
steps.note:
COMPOUND: 비행 (flying) + 기 (machine).
비: to fly. 행: go/travel. 기: machine.
'Flying-travel machine' = airplane.

비행: flying. 비행사: pilot (flying + expert).
공항: airport. 공 (air/public) + 항 (port).

steps.exampleEn: I take the subway every day.
steps.note:
타다: universal 'board/ride' verb for transport.

버스를 타다: take the bus
택시를 타다: take a taxi
자전거를 타다: ride a bicycle
말을 타다: ride a horse

내리다: to get off (opposite of 타다).

steps.title: 🚇 Korean Transport: World Class
steps.text:
Seoul's public transit system:

지하철 (subway):
• 23 lines covering Greater Seoul
• Color-coded, numbered exits
• T-money card works on everything
• Last train ~12-1am

KTX (bullet train):
• Seoul → Busan: 2h 30m
• Seoul → Gwangju: 1h 30m
• Reserved seating, meals available

버스 (bus):
• Color-coded by type:
  파란 버스: trunk routes (blue)
  초록 버스: neighborhood routes (green)
  빨간 버스: express (red)

공항철도: Airport Railroad
Seoul Station → Incheon Airport: 43 min.

steps.hint: Underground + iron/rail

opts: It's an old native Korean word
steps.hint: Flying-machine = airplane
버스를 타다, 기차를 타다

steps.s: 매일 ___ 을/를 타요. (I take the subway every day)
steps.hint: 지하철 = subway (underground + iron)

steps.s: 서울에서 부산까지 ___로 두 시간 삼십 분이에요.
steps.hint: Seoul to Busan 2.5 hours = bullet train
I take the subway every day. On weekends I travel by train.

steps.q: 기 appears in: 비행기, 세탁기, 청소기. 기 means:
steps.hint: 비행기 (flying machine), 세탁기 (washing machine), 청소기 (cleaning machine)

steps.title: P26 Moment: AT (Static) vs AT (Active)
steps.desc: You've seen both 에 and 에서 since Unit 2. Today they get their full names and the distinction between them becomes crystal clear: 에 = where you ARE or GO. 에서 = where you DO things.
goals: Name 에 as destination/static location marker
Name 에서 as action location marker
Apply the correct particle every time

steps.note:
에 marks THREE things:

1. DESTINATION: 학교에 가요 (go TO school)
2. STATIC LOCATION: 집에 있어요 (am AT home)
3. TIME: 3시에 (AT 3 o'clock)

에 answers: WHERE are you? WHERE are you going?
It doesn't say anything about what you DO there.

에서 marks WHERE an ACTION happens.

학교에서 공부해요: study AT school.
카페에서 커피를 마셔요: drink coffee AT the café.
도서관에서 책을 읽어요: read books AT the library.

Test: Is there an ACTION verb?
Yes → 에서
No (just being/going) → 에

steps.title: 📍 에 vs 에서: The Complete System
steps.text:
에 = WHERE something IS or WHERE you GO
에서 = WHERE you DO something

학교에 가요.
→ I go to school. (destination)

학교에 있어요.
→ I'm at school. (static)

학교에서 공부해요.
→ I study at school. (action)

Bonus — 에서 also means FROM:
서울에서 왔어요.
→ I came from Seoul.

에서: WHERE you DO something

집에서 쉬어요: rest AT home (action: resting)
도서관에서 공부해요: study AT library (action: studying)
카페에서 일해요: work AT a café (action: working)

The test:
'What are you doing there?'
If there IS an action → 에서
If you're just BEING there or ARRIVING → 에

Bonus: 에서 also means 'from' in one pattern:
서울에서 왔어요: I came FROM Seoul.

steps.q: 'I study AT the library' — correct particle:
steps.hint: Studying = action at a place → 에서
Being there statically (있어요, no action) → 에
Going TO a destination → 에

steps.q: 'I drink coffee AT the café' — correct particle:
steps.hint: Drinking = action at a place → 에서
Studying is an action — what you DO at the library
Being at home, no action verb — static location
First blank: going TO school (destination)

steps.s: 저는 카페{1} 일해요. 지금 카페{2} 있어요.
steps.hint: I work AT the café (action). Right now I am AT the café (static).
에서 also marks 'from' with movement verbs like 오다

steps.q: 에서 is different from 에 because:
steps.hint: The key distinction: action vs static/destination

steps.title: Going BY Bus, Heading TOWARD the Station
steps.desc: (으)로 does two related jobs: it marks HOW you travel (by bus, by subway) and WHICH DIRECTION you go (to the left, toward the station). One particle, two uses, one concept: direction/means.
goals: Know when to use 로 vs 으로

steps.note:
(으)로: means OR direction.

ROULE:
로: after vowels or ㄹ
으로: after other consonants

지하철로 (지하철 ends in ㄹ → 로)
버스로 (버스 ends in vowel → 로)
학교으로 ✗ → 학교로 ✓ (ends in vowel → 로)

왼쪽으로 (왼쪽 ends in consonant ㄱ → 으로)

(으)로 marks the METHOD of transport:

버스로 가요: go by bus
지하철로 가요: go by subway
비행기로 가요: go by plane
걸어서 가요: go on foot (걸어서 = by walking)

걸어서: special form — 걷다 (walk) + -어서.

(으)로 marks DIRECTION:

왼쪽으로 가세요: go LEFT
오른쪽으로 가세요: go RIGHT
앞으로 가세요: go FORWARD
뒤로 가세요: go BACK
출구 쪽으로 가세요: go toward the exit

steps.hint: 지하철 ends in ㄹ → 로 (not 으로)
BY bus, TOWARD the exit — means and direction
왼쪽 ends in consonant ㄱ → 으로
비행기 ends in vowel → 로
오른쪽 ends in consonant ㄱ → 으로
버스 ends in vowel → 로 (by bus)

steps.s: 직진하다가 왼쪽{1} 가세요. 거기서 버스{2} 가면 돼요.
steps.hint: Go straight, then go left. From there, go by bus.

steps.q: 에 vs (으)로: 학교에 가요 vs 학교로 가요 — the difference:
opts: 에 = AT/TO destination; 로 = heading TOWARD (directional flavor)
steps.ans: 에 = AT/TO destination; 로 = heading TOWARD (directional flavor)
steps.hint: 학교에 가요 (going to school). 학교로 가요 (heading toward school — directional)
걷다 (to walk) + -어서 = by walking

steps.note:
기다리다: to wait.
기다리 + 어요 = 기다려요 (ㅣ+ㅓ = ㅕ).

여기에서 기다리세요: please wait here.
잠깐만 기다려요: wait a moment.
버스를 기다려요: I'm waiting for the bus.

찾다: to find / to look for.
찾 + 아요 = 찾아요 (regular).

길을 찾다: look for the way.
친구를 찾다: look for a friend.
지갑을 찾다: look for a wallet.

찾았어요!: I found it!

(이)랑: and / with (casual version of 하고).

Consonant ending + 이랑: 친구 → X, wait — 친구 ends in vowel → 랑.
Vowel ending + 랑: 친구랑, 엄마랑
Consonant ending + 이랑: 선생님이랑, 동생이랑

친구랑 = 친구하고 (same meaning, more casual).

하고: neutral.
(이)랑: casual/spoken.
와/과: formal/written.

steps.exampleEn: I gave a gift to a friend.
steps.note:
에게: to (a person) — formal/written.
한테: to (a person) — casual/spoken.

친구에게 = 친구한테 (same meaning).

선생님에게 물어봤어요: I asked the teacher.
엄마한테 전화했어요: I called mom.

에: for places/things.
에게/한테: for people/animals.

steps.q: 친구랑 같이 가요. (이)랑 is the casual form of:
steps.hint: (이)랑 = 하고 (and/with). More casual.
에게 (formal) = 한테 (casual). Both mean 'to a person.'

steps.title: Left, Right, Straight, Nearby
steps.desc: Direction vocabulary for real-world navigation. Korea's numbered subway exits make directions simpler. You'll use 으로 from the last lesson constantly here.
goals: Use 어디에 있어요? to find places

steps.note:
쪽: side/direction (reusable suffix).
왼: left. 오른: right.

쪽 reappears everywhere:
앞쪽: front side
뒤쪽: back side
위쪽: upper side
아래쪽: lower side
이쪽: this way (이 + 쪽)

steps.exampleEn: Go straight. Then it's on the left.
steps.note:
직진: go straight.
SINO-KOREAN: 직 (straight) + 진 (advance).

유턴: Konglish for U-turn.

좌회전: left turn (좌=left, 회전=turn)
우회전: right turn (우=right, 회전=turn)

좌우: left-right (direction pair).

근처: nearby area.
근처에: in the vicinity of.

여기 근처에: near here
학교 근처에: near the school
역 근처에: near the station

역: (subway/train) station — important word!

어디: where (question word).
어디에 있어요?: 'Where does it exist?'
= Where is it?

화장실이 어디에 있어요?: Where's the bathroom?
출구가 어디에 있어요?: Where's the exit?
지하철역이 어디에 있어요?: Where's the subway station?

역: station.

홍대역: Hongdae Station
강남역: Gangnam Station
서울역: Seoul Station

역 근처에: near the station
몇 번 출구: which numbered exit

번: number counter (for numbering things).

steps.title: 🚇 Numbered Exits: Korea's Navigation Secret
steps.text:
Every Korean subway station has NUMBERED EXITS.

출구: exit (출=out + 구=opening/mouth)
1번 출구, 2번 출구, 3번 출구...

Why this matters:
Koreans give ALL directions by exit number.

'3번 출구로 나오세요.
→ Please come out exit 3.

홍대역 9번 출구 앞에서 만나요.
→ Let's meet in front of Hongdae Station exit 9.

Practical use:
• Save the exit number, not just the station
• Landmarks are always described by exit number
• Meeting spots are '역 X번 출구 앞' (in front of exit X)

steps.hint: 쪽 = side/direction suffix. 이쪽, 앞쪽, 뒤쪽 all use it.
어디에 있어요? = Where does it exist? = Where is it?

steps.q: 직진 = 직 + 진. What does it mean?
steps.hint: 직 (straight) + 진 (advance)
'3번 출구로 나오세요' — exit number is the landmark

steps.s: 지하철역이 ___ 있어요? (Where is the subway station?)
steps.hint: 어디에 있어요 = where is it? (어디 + location particle 에)
오른쪽 ends in consonant ㄱ → 으로
근처 = nearby/vicinity
Go straight, then go left. It's near the station.

steps.q: 'Let's meet in front of exit 9 at Hongdae Station':
steps.hint: 역 + numbered exit + 앞에서 + 만나요 — standard Korean meeting arrangement

steps.exampleEn: It's on top of the desk.
steps.note:
위: above, on top.
위에: on top of / above (+ 에 for location).

책상 위에: on the desk.
냉장고 위에: on top of the fridge.

위 ↔ 아래/밑: top ↔ bottom.

옆: beside, next to.
옆에: next to / beside.

학교 옆에: next to the school.
내 옆에 앉아요: sit next to me.

방향(direction) vs 위치(position):
왼쪽으로 가세요 (direction) vs 옆에 있어요 (position).

밖: outside.
밖에: outside of.

안 (inside) ↔ 밖 (outside).

밖에 나가요: go outside.
문 밖에: outside the door.
집 밖에: outside the house.

steps.exampleEn: It's between the bank and the café.
steps.note:
사이: between.
A와 B 사이에: between A and B.

은행과 카페 사이에: between the bank and café.

사이 also means 'relationship':
우리 사이: our relationship / between us.

steps.title: 📍 The Complete 8-Position Set
steps.text:
You now know all the core position words:

위 (above) ↔ 아래/밑 (below)
앞 (front) ↔ 뒤 (back)
안 (inside) ↔ 밖 (outside)
왼쪽 (left) ↔ 오른쪽 (right)

Plus:
옆 (beside/next to)
사이 (between)
근처 (nearby)

All use +에 있어요 to say 'is at [position]':

책상 위에 있어요.
→ It's on top of the desk.

은행 옆에 있어요.
→ It's next to the bank.

집 밖에 있어요.
→ It's outside the house.

steps.note:
건너다: to cross.
건너가다: to go across.

길을 건너다: cross the road.
다리를 건너다: cross the bridge.
횡단보도: crosswalk.

건너세요: please cross (polite command).

돌다: to turn.
돌아가다: to turn and go.

왼쪽으로 돌아가세요: turn left.
오른쪽으로 돌아가세요: turn right.

좌회전/우회전: formal signs.
돌아가세요 = conversational direction.

steps.hint: 옆 = beside/next to
A와 B 사이에 = between A and B
안 = inside, 밖 = outside
건너다 = to cross

steps.s: 책상 ___ 에 책이 있어요. (The book is on top of the desk)
steps.hint: 위 = above/on top

steps.s: 직진하다가 왼쪽으로 ___. (Go straight then turn left)
steps.hint: 돌다/돌아가다 = to turn

steps.title: The Full Engine — Contractions and What's Coming
steps.desc: You know the stem. You know vowel harmony. Now: the full set of contraction patterns, and a first look at the irregular families coming in Unit 7. Consider this your A1 verb graduation.
goals: See every verb class you've used in A1 systematically
Plant seeds for the 5 irregular families

steps.title: 📋 The Complete Contraction Map
steps.text:
When the stem's final vowel meets the -아/어요 ending, these contacts happen:

CONTRACTIONS:
가 + 아요 = 가요 (ㅏ+ㅏ → single ㅏ)
오 + 아요 = 와요 (ㅗ+ㅏ → ㅘ)
보 + 아요 = 봐요 (ㅗ+ㅏ → ㅘ)
마시 + 어요 = 마셔요 (ㅣ+ㅓ → ㅕ)
배우 + 어요 = 배워요 (ㅜ+ㅓ → ㅝ)
쓰 + 어요 = 써요 (ㅡ drops, ㅓ takes over)
하 + 아요 = 해요 (ㅏ+ㅏ → ㅐ: special)

NO CONTRACTION (consonant stems — vowel inserted):
먹 + 어요 = 먹어요
있 + 어요 = 있어요
읽 + 어요 = 읽어요

steps.q: 배우다 (to learn) — stem 배우, vowel ㅜ. Polite form?
steps.hint: ㅜ+ㅓ contracts to ㅝ. 배우 + 어요 = 배워요

steps.q: 쓰다 (to write/use) — stem 쓰, vowel ㅡ. Polite form?
steps.hint: ㅡ drops when followed by a vowel ending. 쓰 + 어요 = 써요

steps.q: 읽다 (to read) — stem 읽, consonant. Polite form?
steps.hint: Consonant stem + 어요. No contraction. 읽 + 어요 = 읽어요

steps.title: ⚡ The 5 Irregular Families — JRTF
steps.text:
Most Korean verbs are completely regular.
But 5 families behave differently when vowel endings attach.
You've already seen some of them — now you know to watch for them.

① ㄷ-IRREGULAR: ㄷ becomes ㄹ before vowels
걷다 (walk) → 걸어요 (not 걷어요)
듣다 (listen) → 들어요

② ㅂ-IRREGULAR: ㅂ becomes 우 before vowels
덥다 (hot) → 더워요
춥다 (cold) → 추워요
어렵다 (difficult) → 어려워요

③ ㅅ-IRREGULAR: ㅅ drops before vowels
낫다 (to get better) → 나아요

④ 르-IRREGULAR: 르 doubles ㄹ
모르다 (not know) → 몰라요
부르다 (call) → 불러요

⑤ ㅎ-IRREGULAR: ㅎ drops/transforms
빨갛다 (red) → 빨개요
하얗다 (white) → 하얘요

⚡ JRTF: Full explanation in Unit 7 (①②) and Unit 9 (④⑤).
For now: notice the pattern. You've heard 더워요 and 어려워요 before.

deepDive.title: Why Do Irregulars Exist?
deepDive.text:
Korean irregulars are not random.
They're the result of historical sound changes:

ㄷ-irregular: in old Korean, ㄷ softened to ㄹ before vowels.
Regular ㄷ verbs like 받다 (receive) didn't change.
Irregular ones like 듣다 (listen) did. Inherited from the sound shift.

ㅂ-irregular: ㅂ was originally a bilabial fricative (like English 'v').
Before vowels, it weakened to 오/우.
Hence: 덥다 → 더워요 (더 + v + 어요 → 더우어요 → 더워요)

These aren't exceptions to memorise — they're historical fossils.
Knowing the pattern lets you predict them.

steps.q: 덥다 (hot) is a ㅂ-irregular. Its polite form is:
steps.hint: ㅂ-irregular: ㅂ → 우 before vowels. 덥 → 더우 + 어요 = 더워요

steps.q: 모르다 (not know) is a 르-irregular. Its polite form is:
steps.hint: 르-irregular: 르 → ㄹ doubles. 모르 + 아요 = 몰라요

steps.note:
The past tense adds ONE car between the stem and -요:

가 (bright ㅏ) + 았어요 = 갔어요
먹 (dark ㅓ) + 었어요 = 먹었어요
하 → 했어요 (always)

⚡ JRTF: Same vowel harmony rule applies to past!
Bright stem → -았어요
Dark stem → -었어요
하다 → 했어요

This is Unit 7. You're ready. Just notice the pattern now.

steps.hint: Dark vowel → 었어요. 먹 + 었어요 = 먹었어요

steps.q: 가다 (bright vowel ㅏ) — past tense form:
steps.hint: Bright vowel → 았어요. 가 + 았어요 = 갔어요 (contraction: ㅏ+ㅏ=ㅏ)
하다 always: 해요 (present), 했어요 (past). Special contraction.

steps.title: 🎓 A1 Verb Graduation
steps.text:
You leave A1 knowing:

🚂 Stem + ending = every Korean verb
⚡ Bright (ㅏ/ㅗ) → -아요 / Dark → -어요
🎙️ 해요체 = your register
📋 All contraction patterns
🌱 5 irregular families (to be explained in A2)
⏰ Past tense preview: -았/었어요

Unit 7 formally teaches the past tense.
You already know the engine. You just need the new car.

Welcome to A2.

steps.title: Hot, Cold, and JRTF Irregular Forms
steps.desc: Weather is day-one practical vocabulary. But several key weather words use irregular conjugation. Flag them now — Unit 7 will explain the ㅂ pattern.
goals: Say hot, cold, sunny, rainy, cloudy
Accept JRTF for ㅂ-irregular weather verbs

steps.note:
날씨: weather.
날: day/weather. 씨: suffix.

날씨가 어때요?: How's the weather?
어때요?: 'How is it?' — very useful question pattern.

덥다: to be hot (weather/temperature).

더워요 (NOT 덥어요!).
The ㅂ at the end changes to 워 before vowel endings.

⚡ JUST REMEMBER THIS FORM.
Full ㅂ-irregular pattern: Unit 7.

춥다: to be cold (weather/temperature).

추워요 (NOT 춥어요!).
Same ㅂ-irregular as 덥다 (hot).
추워요 (NOT 춥어요).

⚡ JUST REMEMBER THIS FORM.
겨울: winter. 여름: summer.

맑다: clear, sunny.
맑아요: regular! (no irregular change)

날씨가 맑아요: the weather is clear
하늘이 맑아요: the sky is clear

하늘: sky. 하늘색: sky-blue (하늘 + 색).

비: rain.
와요: comes (비가 오다 = rain comes)

Korean thinks of rain, snow as 'coming':
비가 와요: rain comes = it rains
눈이 와요: snow comes = it snows

비가 많이 와요: it rains a lot
비가 조금 와요: it rains a little

흐리다: cloudy.
흐려요: it's cloudy (이 + 어 → 여 contraction).

Same pattern you saw with 마시다 → 마셔요.
이 + 어 → 여

맑음: clear (weather forecast)
흐림: cloudy (weather forecast)
비: rain (weather forecast)

steps.hint: ⚡ JRTF: 덥다 → 더워요 (ㅂ-irregular)
Korean thinks of rain as 'coming': 비가 오다
어때요 = how is it? 날씨 = weather

steps.q: 춥다 → 추워요 is an example of:
steps.hint: JRTF: just remember the form, pattern in Unit 7

steps.s: 오늘 날씨가 ___. 비가 와요. (It's cloudy today. It's raining.)
steps.hint: 흐리다 → 흐려요 (이 + 어 → 여)

steps.s: 여름에 ___, 겨울에 추워요. (In summer it's hot, in winter it's cold)
steps.hint: ⚡ JRTF: 덥다 → 더워요
It's cloudy today. It's raining.
눈: snow. 눈이 오다 = snow comes = it snows. Same pattern as 비가 와요.
하늘 (sky) + 색 (color) = sky-blue color

steps.note:
마다: every / each.

날마다: every day (= 매일).
주마다: every week.
사람마다: every person / each person.

매일 vs 날마다: same meaning.
매 (每: every) is Sino-Korean.
마다 is pure Korean particle.

steps.exampleEn: The weather is very good today.
steps.note:
아주: very, extremely.

아주 좋아요: very good.
아주 맛있어요: very delicious.

아주 vs 너무 vs 정말:
아주: very (neutral emphasis)
너무: too much (can be positive or negative)
정말: really (emphasis on truth)

steps.example: 바람이 불어요. 산이 높아요. 바다가 넓어요.
steps.exampleEn: The wind blows. The mountain is high. The sea is wide.
steps.note:
자연 (nature) vocabulary:

바람: wind. 바람이 불다: wind blows.
산 (山): mountain. 산이 높아요: the mountain is high.
바다: sea/ocean. 바다가 넓어요: the sea is wide.
나무: tree. 나무가 많아요: there are many trees.
강 (江): river. 한강: Han River.

등산: mountain climbing (登山: climb + mountain).
등산하다: to go hiking.

steps.hint: 마다 = every/each. 날마다 = every day.
아주 = very/extremely.

steps.title: Compound Words + Korean Transit Culture
steps.desc: A rapid-fire compound decoding session with transport words, then the cultural anchor: the Han River as the heart of Seoul social life.

steps.note:
COMPOUND: 버스 + 정류 + 장.
정류: stop/stay. 정 (stop/fix) + 류 (flow).
'Stop the flow' = stopping place.
장: place.

버스 정류장: 'bus stopping-place'
주차장: parking lot (주차 + 장: parking place)
화장실: bathroom (화장 + 실: makeup-clean room)

steps.exampleEn: How do I get to the airport?
steps.note:
SINO-KOREAN: 공항 = 공 + 항.
공 (空): air / public / empty. 항 (港): port.
'Air port' = airport.

공기: air (공 + 기 = air + energy/matter)
공원: park (공 + 원 = public + garden)
공장: factory (공 + 장 = manufacturing + place)

SINO-KOREAN: 출 + 구.
출 (出): to go out. 구 (口): mouth/opening.
'Going-out opening' = exit.

출 reappears in:
출근: going to work (출 + 근: out + attendance)
출발: departure (출 + 발: out + departure)

나오세요: please come out (나오다 + 세요)

steps.exampleEn: I ride using the T-money card.
steps.note:
T-money: Korea's rechargeable transit card.

지하철, 버스, 택시 — all accept T-money.
Buy at any 편의점 (convenience store).
Top up at subway machines.

카드를 찍다: tap the card
요금: fare (교통 요금: transit fare)

steps.title: 🌉 한강: Seoul's Social Heart
steps.text:
한강 = Han River — runs through the center of Seoul.
한 (韓) = Korea / great. 강 (江) = river.

한국 사람의 거실
→ Korean people's living room.

What people do at 한강:
• 치킨 + 맥주 (chicken + beer) on the grass
• 자전거 타기 (cycling) on riverside paths
• 야경 감상 (night view) at 63빌딩
• 한강 유람선 (cruise boat)
• 피크닉 (picnic) with convenience store food

한강 치킨 배달:
You can order chicken delivery to the riverbank.
Delivery person brings it right to you.

한강 culture = 정 (shared life) + 빨리빨리 (fast-paced, efficient fun)

'한강 가자!' = Let's go to the Han River!
= Let's hang out.

steps.hint: 공 (air) + 항 (port) = airport
출 (出): to go out. 출근 (going to work), 출발 (departure)

steps.q: 버스 정류장 = 버스 + 정류 + 장. 장 means:
steps.hint: 장 = place. 주차장 (parking place), 시장 (market place)
T-money is the universal Korean transit payment system

steps.s: ___ 에 어떻게 가요? (How do I get to the airport?)
steps.hint: 공 (air) + 항 (port) = airport
출구 = exit (出口)
From Seoul Station, I go to Busan by KTX.

opts: Delivery chicken to the Han River bank
steps.ans: Delivery chicken to the Han River bank
steps.hint: You can have chicken delivered directly to you at the riverbank — 정 + convenience culture

steps.title: The Final A1 Toolkit
steps.desc: 못 (can't), -지만 (but), four connectors, and frequency adverbs. These appear in every Korean conversation from day one.

steps.note:
못 goes before the verb.

못 가요
→ can't go

못 먹어요
→ can't eat

못 자요
→ can't sleep

steps.title: 💡 수영을 못해요 — why 을?
steps.text:
수영하다 = 수영 (swimming) + 하다 (to do)
(to swim = swimming + to do)

With 하다 verbs, 못 splits the compound:

수영을 못해요
→ (I) can't swim

운전을 못해요
→ (I) can't drive

한국어를 못해요
→ (I) can't speak Korean

With plain verbs, 못 just goes before:

못 가요
→ can't go

못 먹어요
→ can't eat

steps.example: 오늘 안 가요.  /  오늘 못 가요.
steps.exampleEn: I'm not going today (choice).  /  I can't go today (inability).
steps.note:
안 = I choose not to.
못 = I am not able to.

Using the wrong one changes what you're saying.

steps.title: 🚫 안 vs 못 — The Difference Matters
steps.text:
매운 음식을 안 먹어요.
→ I don't eat spicy food. (preference)

매운 음식을 못 먹어요.
→ I can't eat spicy food. (physically unable)

커피를 안 마셔요.
→ I don't drink coffee. (choice)

커피를 못 마셔요.
→ I can't drink coffee. (e.g. allergy)

steps.q: 'I can't eat spicy food (not capable)' — correct:
steps.hint: 못 = not capable
안 = I choose not to

steps.s: 저는 운전을 ___해요. (I can't drive — not capable)
steps.hint: Inability = 못

steps.note:
COMPOUND: -지 (verb connector) + 못하다 (cannot do).
Literally: 'verb-and-cannot'

수영을 못해요 = 수영하지 못해요
→ Both mean: can't swim
→ -지 못하다 is slightly more formal

steps.exampleEn: Korean is difficult, but it's interesting.
steps.note:
COMPOUND: stem + 지만 (but/however).

어렵다 → 어렵지만
→ (to be) difficult → but difficult

가다 → 가지만
→ to go → but going

비싸다 → 비싸지만
→ (to be) expensive → but expensive

steps.title: ✂️ -지만 in Action
steps.text:
비싸지만 맛있어요.
→ It's expensive, but delicious.

가고 싶지만 시간이 없어요.
→ I want to go, but I have no time.

어렵지만 재미있어요.
→ It's hard, but interesting.

steps.hint: -지만 = but (contrast)
-지만 attaches to the first verb stem

steps.exampleEn: I'm tired. So I'm staying home.
steps.note:
Connects two sentences: reason → result.
그래서 starts the SECOND sentence.

배가 고파요. 그래서 밥을 먹었어요.
→ I'm hungry. So I ate.

Two uses:

1. Contrast:
맛있어요. 그런데 비싸요.
→ It's delicious. But it's expensive.

2. Topic shift:
그런데, 오늘 뭐 했어요?
→ By the way, what did you do today?

근데 = casual spoken form

그럼요!
→ Of course! (very common polite reply)

그럼 = casual spoken form of 그러면

steps.exampleEn: Coffee or tea? What would you like?
steps.note:
COMPOUND: 아니다 (not be) + (으)면 (if-condition).
Literally: 'if it is not (that)'

커피 아니면 차
→ coffee or tea

여기 아니면 거기
→ here or there

steps.title: 🔗 The Connector Set — A1 Complete
steps.text:
WITHIN a sentence:

-고: and/then
먹고 마셔요 → eat, then drink

-지만: but
어렵지만 재미있어요 → hard but interesting

-아/어서: because → Unit 9

BETWEEN sentences:

그리고 → and also
그래서 → so / therefore
그런데 / 근데 → but / by the way
그러면 / 그럼 → then / in that case
아니면 → or / otherwise

Half these keep appearing all the way to B1.

steps.q: 'I was tired. So I stayed home.' — which connector?
steps.hint: 그래서 = so/therefore (reason → result)
아니면 = or / otherwise
그런데 = but / by the way

steps.exampleEn: I often exercise. I sometimes watch movies.
steps.note:
Adverbs go BEFORE the verb:

자주 운동해요 ✓
→ I often exercise. (correct)

운동해요 자주 ✗
→ wrong word order

Frequency scale:
항상 (always) → 자주 (often) → 보통 (usually) → 가끔 (sometimes)

steps.exampleEn: It's not really good. I have no idea at all.
steps.note:
Both ALWAYS pair with a negative:

별로 안 좋아요
→ not really good

전혀 없어요
→ absolutely none

전혀 몰라요
→ I have absolutely no idea

다시 한 번만요.
→ Just one more time.

혼자 왔어요.
→ I came alone.

천천히 말해 주세요.
→ Please speak slowly.

steps.exampleEn: Why? How long does it take?
steps.note:
왜요?
→ Why? (casual)

얼마나 걸려요?
→ How long does it take?

Full question word set — now complete:
뭐 (what) | 어디 (where) | 언제 (when)
어떻게 (how) | 왜 (why) | 누가 (who)
얼마 (how much — price) | 얼마나 (how long/much — degree) | 몇 (how many)

steps.exampleEn: I like it the most. / It's the best.
steps.note:
SINO-KOREAN: 제 (第: rank) + 일 (一: one) = number one = the most.

제일 좋아요
→ I like it the most.

가장 어려워요
→ It's the most difficult.

제일 = 가장 — same meaning, 제일 more common in speech.

steps.title: 📊 Frequency Scale — Full A1 Set
steps.text:
항상 / 늘
→ always

자주
→ often

보통
→ usually

가끔
→ sometimes

별로 (+ negative)
→ not really

전혀 (+ negative)
→ not at all

이제 A1이 진짜 끝났어요.
→ Now A1 is truly complete.

steps.hint: 별로 안 좋아요, 전혀 없어요 — always negative

steps.q: 'How long does it take?' in Korean:
steps.hint: 얼마나 = how much/long (degree/duration)

steps.s: 저는 ___ 커피를 마셔요. (I often drink coffee)
steps.hint: 자주 = often
천천히 = slowly
I often exercise. So I'm tired. I sometimes rest.

steps.title: Unit 6 Complete — A1 Done
steps.desc: Transport, particles, directions, connectors, negation, frequency adverbs. A1 is complete. Everything TOPIK 1 requires is now in place.
goals: Verify 에 vs 에서 vs (으)로 mastery
Test connector and frequency adverb knowledge

steps.example: 저는 버스로 학교에 가요. 학교에서 공부해요. 어렵지만 재미있어요.
steps.exampleEn: I go to school by bus. I study at school. It's hard but interesting.
steps.note:
PARTICLES: 은/는, 이/가, 을/를, 에, 에서, (으)로, 의, 도 — all named.

NEGATION: 안 (choice), 못 (inability), -지 않아요, -지 못해요.

CONNECTORS: -고, -지만, 그래서, 그런데, 그러면, 아니면.

FREQUENCY: 자주, 가끔, 보통, 별로, 전혀, 왜, 얼마나, 제일/가장.

POSITIONS: 위/아래/앞/뒤/안/밖/옆/사이 — complete set.

VERB STRUCTURE: stem + ending (preview). Full system Unit 7.

✅ You are TOPIK 1 ready.

steps.q: 에 vs 에서 — the key difference:
steps.hint: 에 = being/going there. 에서 = doing something there.
지하 (underground) + 철 (iron/rail)
BY bus (means), TOWARD the right (direction)
Strip 다 from 먹다. Remaining = stem.
⚡ JRTF: 덥다 → 더워요 (ㅂ-irregular, Unit 7)
공 (空): air, public, empty. 공기, 공원, 공항

steps.q: 비가 와요 — why 와요 (come) not another verb?
steps.hint: 비가 오다 = rain comes = it rains. 눈이 오다 = snow comes = it snows.
Studying is an ACTION at the library → 에서

steps.s: 지하철___학교에 가요. (I go to school by subway)
steps.hint: 지하철 ends in ㄹ → 로 (by subway)
오다 → 오 + 아 contracts to 와. 와요.

steps.s: 저는 버스{1} 공항{2} 가요. 공항{3} 두 시간 기다렸어요.
steps.hint: I go to the airport by bus. I waited at the airport for 2 hours.

steps.q: You've now finished A1.2. You can:
opts: Handle survival Korean situations and navigate Korea
steps.ans: Handle survival Korean situations and navigate Korea
steps.hint: A1.2 = survival level. You can navigate, eat, shop, ask directions, handle numbers.
